frames.name: Corporate Crisis Management
frames.description: Focuses on OpenAI's rushed decision-making and subsequent damage control efforts
frames.emphasis: OpenAI's admission of poor timing, user backlash, app uninstalls, and Altman's acknowledgment of being 'opportunistic and sloppy'

frames.name: Legal/Technical Safeguards Analysis
frames.description: Examines the contractual differences between OpenAI and Anthropic's approaches to Pentagon deals
frames.emphasis: OpenAI's reliance on existing laws versus Anthropic's demand for specific contractual prohibitions, questioning adequacy of legal protections

frames.name: Market Competition and Branding
frames.description: Views the controversy through lens of business strategy and market positioning
frames.emphasis: AI models as commodified products, Anthropic's moral branding strategy, potential market value of taking principled stance

frames.name: Democratic Governance vs Corporate Power
frames.description: Frames debate around who should control AI policy - elected officials or private companies
frames.emphasis: Altman's deference to democratic process, tech industry's unpreparedness for national security role

frames.name: Authoritarian Drift Warning
frames.description: Presents Pentagon's actions as potentially undermining democratic values the US claims to defend
frames.emphasis: Comparison to Chinese authoritarianism, questioning whether US is becoming what it opposes

frames.name: Employee Resistance Movement
frames.description: Highlights tech worker organizing against military AI applications
frames.emphasis: Cross-company solidarity, employee letters, broader context of tech worker activism

outlierPerspectives: Anthropic's participation in Pentagon drone swarming competition despite public stance (Bloomberg/CNBC video)
Continued use of Claude in Iran operations despite blacklisting (BBC World, CNBC video)
Framing Pentagon as 'Department of War' in employee letters (CNBC)
Suggestion that losing Pentagon contracts may benefit Anthropic's brand (The Guardian US)
missingContext: Specific details of what triggered the Iran military operations and their relationship to AI policy timing
Financial value of the Pentagon contracts at stake
Technical capabilities that make these AI models specifically valuable for military applications
Historical precedent for Pentagon designating US companies as 'supply chain risks'
Details of other AI companies' (Google, Microsoft, Meta) current Pentagon relationships
Broader context of US-China AI competition beyond rhetorical claims
Legal analysis of whether existing laws actually prevent mass surveillance and autonomous weapons
Timeline and details of how Anthropic's relationship with Pentagon deteriorated
